explanation.summary: PTSD treatment guidelines universally recommend trauma-focused psychological therapy as first-line. TF-CBT includes gradual exposure, cognitive restructuring, and trauma narrative development. Pharmacotherapy with SSRIs is second-line. Critical incident debriefing is contraindicated.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT is first-line for PTSD
SSRIs are second-line when psychotherapy is unavailable or insufficient
Psychological debriefing is contraindicated
Non-trauma-focused therapies are inferior
explanation.pearls: EMDR has equivalent efficacy to TF-CBT for PTSD and is recommended as an alternative first-line therapy by NICE and Phoenix Australia - the choice depends on patient preference and therapist availability.

subtopic_detail: Complex PTSD phased treatment approach
stem: A 29-year-old woman is referred for persistent trauma symptoms following years of childhood sexual abuse. She has difficulties with emotional regulation, oscillating between rage and dissociation. She has unstable relationships and chronic feelings of emptiness and shame. She frequently dissociates under stress. She has been diagnosed with BPD but her psychologist believes complex PTSD is more appropriate. She has attempted standard TF-CBT twice before but disengaged both times after becoming overwhelmed during exposure components.

data_table.Examination: Dysphoric affect, episodes of dissociation during interview, no active suicidal ideation, insight present

lead_in: What is the most appropriate therapeutic approach?

options.text: Restart standard prolonged exposure therapy with increased session frequency
options.rationale: This patient has twice disengaged from standard exposure therapy. Repeating the approach that caused decompensation is clinically inappropriate. A phased model is needed. (Source: ISTSS: Complex PTSD Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Commence phase-based treatment with stabilisation skills training before any trauma processing
options.rationale: Complex PTSD from prolonged interpersonal trauma often presents with emotional dysregulation and dissociation beyond core PTSD. The expert consensus is phase-based treatment: Phase 1 stabilisation and affect regulation skills (drawing from DBT), Phase 2 trauma processing, Phase 3 social reconnection. This patient's dissociation and prior decompensation mandate a longer stabilisation phase. (Source: ISTSS: Complex PTSD Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe venlafaxine 225 mg daily and defer all psychological therapy until mood stabilises
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy alone is insufficient for complex PTSD. The stabilisation phase of psychological therapy should begin alongside any pharmacological intervention. (Source: Phoenix Australia: PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for intensive EMDR therapy with daily sessions over a 2-week block
options.rationale: Intensive daily sessions are inappropriate for this patient. Her dissociation and decompensation history indicate she needs stabilisation first. Intensive protocols are for single-event trauma with good affect regulation. (Source: ISTSS: Complex PTSD Treatment Guidelines)

options.text: Commence sodium valproate for mood instability and group interpersonal therapy
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is for bipolar disorder, not complex PTSD. Emotional dysregulation in complex PTSD is better addressed through DBT skills training. Group therapy may eventually help but is not appropriate as initial intervention. (Source: RANZCP CPG: PTSD)

explanation.summary: Complex PTSD (ICD-11) from prolonged interpersonal trauma adds emotional dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal disturbance to core PTSD symptoms. Phase-based treatment is the expert consensus: stabilisation with affect regulation skills, then trauma processing, then social reconnection. Patients with prominent dissociation often cannot tolerate standard exposure until they have acquired regulation skills.
key_points: Complex PTSD adds emotional dysregulation, negative self-concept, and interpersonal disturbance
Phase-based: stabilisation, trauma processing, social reconnection
Standard exposure may cause decompensation in patients with prominent dissociation
DBT skills training during stabilisation builds affect regulation capacity
explanation.pearls: The ISTSS recommends that stabilisation phase duration be individualised - in complex PTSD with severe dissociation, this phase may last 6-12 months before trauma processing begins.

subtopic_detail: Family-based treatment for adolescent anorexia nervosa
stem: A 15-year-old girl is diagnosed with anorexia nervosa. She has lost 8 kg over 6 months and her BMI is 16.2 kg/m2. She is medically stable with normal vital signs and electrolytes. She lives with both parents and a younger sibling. She is resistant to eating but her parents are engaged and motivated. This is her first eating disorder presentation.

data_table.Investigations: Na+ 140, K+ 3.8, Mg2+ 0.85, phosphate 1.1, ECG normal sinus bradycardia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line outpatient treatment?

options.text: Individual CBT-E with the adolescent focusing on dietary self-monitoring
options.rationale: CBT-E is evidence-based for adults but NOT first-line for adolescent anorexia nervosa. Family-based treatment has the strongest evidence in this age group. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg daily with dietary counselling from a dietitian alone
options.rationale: SSRIs have no evidence base for anorexia nervosa treatment. A dietitian is part of the team but counselling alone is insufficient. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Family-based treatment (Maudsley approach) with the parents managing refeeding
options.rationale: FBT is the strongest evidence-based treatment for adolescent AN. Phase 1: parents take control of eating; Phase 2: gradual return of autonomy; Phase 3: broader developmental issues. FBT achieves remission in 40-50% by end of treatment. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Inpatient admission for supervised refeeding with nasogastric supplementation
options.rationale: This patient is medically stable. Inpatient admission is reserved for medical instability (severe bradycardia below 50, electrolyte derangement, hypothermia). First-line outpatient treatment should be attempted first. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Adolescent-focused psychodynamic therapy with fortnightly family sessions
options.rationale: AFT has some evidence but is less effective than FBT in head-to-head trials. With engaged parents, FBT is clearly the first choice. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Eating Disorders)

explanation.summary: FBT is the gold-standard outpatient treatment for adolescent AN. It externalises the illness, removes blame, and empowers parents to manage refeeding. SSRIs have no role in AN treatment.
key_points: FBT is first-line for adolescent AN per RANZCP and NICE guidelines
FBT externalises the illness and empowers parents
SSRIs have no evidence base for AN
Inpatient admission is reserved for medical instability
explanation.pearls: FBT achieves full remission in 40-50% of adolescents at end of treatment, with 75% in full remission at 5-year follow-up in the original Maudsley trial.

subtopic_detail: Family intervention in first-episode psychosis
stem: A 21-year-old man is recovering from a first episode of psychosis. He has been stable on risperidone 3 mg daily for 3 months and is living with his parents. His mother is highly anxious and frequently checks on him, while his father is critical and frustrated about the patient's lack of motivation. The early psychosis team notes high expressed emotion. The patient is engaged in individual CBT for psychosis.

data_table.Examination: Mild residual negative symptoms, no positive symptoms, good rapport with treating team

lead_in: Which additional intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing relapse?

options.text: Switch risperidone to clozapine for superior long-term relapse prevention
options.rationale: Clozapine is for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failure of two adequate trials). This patient is stable. The modifiable risk factor here is high expressed emotion. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Increase risperidone to 6 mg daily to reduce residual negative symptoms
options.rationale: Increasing antipsychotic dose does not improve negative symptoms and may worsen them through increased sedation and EPS. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Add sodium valproate as an adjunctive mood stabiliser for irritability
options.rationale: The irritability is the father's frustration, not the patient's symptom. Adding medication to the patient does not address family dynamics. Family intervention is the evidence-based approach. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Commence structured family intervention for psychoeducation and communication
options.rationale: High expressed emotion is one of the strongest predictors of psychotic relapse. Family intervention programs reduce relapse by approximately 50% compared to standard care alone. They include psychoeducation, communication skills training, problem-solving, and reduction of expressed emotion. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Advise the patient to move out of the family home to reduce interpersonal stress
options.rationale: Advising a recovering young person to leave the family is practically unrealistic and therapeutically counterproductive. The appropriate intervention is to modify the family dynamic through structured intervention. (Source: NICE: Psychosis and Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: High expressed emotion (criticism, hostility, over-involvement) is one of the strongest modifiable predictors of psychotic relapse. Family intervention programs reduce relapse by approximately 50%. They include psychoeducation, communication skills training, and problem-solving.
key_points: High expressed emotion predicts psychotic relapse
Family intervention reduces relapse by approximately 50%
Programs include psychoeducation, communication skills, and problem-solving
Increasing antipsychotic dose does not improve negative symptoms
explanation.pearls: Patients returning to high-EE households have a 9-month relapse rate of approximately 50% versus 20% in low-EE households - making family EE a stronger predictor of relapse than medication non-adherence.

subtopic_detail: Recovery-oriented practice principles
stem: A 38-year-old man with schizophrenia has been stable on paliperidone depot for 2 years. He lives in supported accommodation and attends a day program. At a care review, he expresses a strong desire to get a part-time job and move to independent housing. His case manager is concerned about relapse risk.

data_table.Examination: Stable mental state, mild residual negative symptoms, good insight, adherent to medication

lead_in: Which approach best reflects recovery-oriented practice?

options.text: Advise him to remain in current accommodation and consolidate his stability further
options.rationale: Prioritising clinician-determined stability over the patient's goals contradicts recovery-oriented principles. Risk-taking towards meaningful goals is part of recovery. (Source: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services)
source_refs: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

options.text: Set a 12-month minimum stability period before considering any changes
options.rationale: Arbitrary time-based thresholds are not evidence-based and are paternalistic. He has been stable for 2 years already. (Source: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services)
source_refs: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

options.text: Refer him for a capacity assessment to determine if he can make this decision
options.rationale: A capacity assessment is not indicated. He has insight, is adherent, and is expressing reasonable goals. Requesting capacity assessment for normal life goals is stigmatising. (Source: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services)
source_refs: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

options.text: Support his employment goal but advise against moving from supported accommodation
options.rationale: Selectively supporting some goals while blocking others based on clinician comfort is not recovery-oriented. Both goals deserve collaborative planning. (Source: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services)
source_refs: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

options.text: Collaboratively develop a graduated plan for employment and independent living
options.rationale: Recovery-oriented practice centres on the person's goals supported by collaborative planning. The National Framework identifies self-determination, person-centred care, supported risk-taking, and hope. The plan should include vocational rehabilitation, IPS program, staged housing transition, and relapse prevention planning. (Source: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services)
source_refs: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

explanation.summary: Recovery-oriented practice prioritises the person's goals as the central organising principle of care. The Australian National Framework outlines self-determination, individually tailored support, and supported risk-taking. IPS is an evidence-based supported employment model with 55% competitive employment rates.
key_points: Recovery is defined by the person, not the clinician
Supported risk-taking is a core principle
IPS achieves competitive employment rates of 55%
Graduated transition with contingency planning is preferred
explanation.pearls: IPS programs achieve competitive employment rates of 55% compared to 25% for traditional vocational rehabilitation - key principles are rapid job search, integration with mental health treatment, and ongoing in-work support.

sources.label: National Framework for Recovery-Oriented Mental Health Services

subtopic_detail: Supported decision making and psychiatric advance directives
stem: A 42-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is being treated voluntarily during a depressive episode. She has capacity and is engaged in treatment planning. She wants to create a document outlining her treatment preferences for future manic episodes, including which medications she consents to, which she refuses, and who she nominates to be involved in decisions if she loses capacity.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic at current review, good insight, capacity confirmed

lead_in: What is this document called and how should the psychiatrist respond?

options.text: This is a psychiatric advance directive; support its completion and document it in clinical records
options.rationale: A psychiatric advance directive allows a person to document treatment preferences while they have capacity, for future episodes when capacity may be impaired. The CRPD and Australian mental health legislation increasingly support advance directives. The psychiatrist should facilitate completion, discuss clinical implications, and document prominently. While not absolutely binding in all jurisdictions, it must be given significant weight. (Source: RANZCP: Advance Statements)

options.text: This is a guardianship application; refer to the state guardianship tribunal
options.rationale: Guardianship appoints a substitute decision maker for someone who lacks capacity. This patient HAS capacity and is planning proactively. She does not need a guardian. (Source: RANZCP: Advance Statements)

options.text: This is inappropriate; treatment decisions during a manic episode must be made by the treating team
options.rationale: Refusing to support an advance directive is contrary to supported decision making principles, the CRPD, and contemporary mental health legislation. Advance directives must be given significant weight. (Source: UN CRPD)

options.text: This is an enduring power of attorney; refer to a solicitor for legal drafting
options.rationale: An EPOA allows a nominated person to make decisions on behalf. What she describes - documenting her OWN preferences - is an advance directive, which can be completed with the treating psychiatrist. (Source: RANZCP: Advance Statements)

options.text: Advise against completing this document as it may compromise her future clinical care
options.rationale: Discouraging advance directives is contrary to recovery-oriented principles and the CRPD. They improve engagement and reduce coercive treatment. (Source: RANZCP: Advance Statements)

explanation.summary: Psychiatric advance directives allow people with episodic mental illness to document treatment preferences while they have capacity. They are a key tool of supported decision making, consistent with the CRPD. They improve treatment satisfaction and reduce involuntary treatment.
key_points: PADs document treatment preferences while the person has capacity
They are a supported decision making tool consistent with the CRPD
They should be documented in clinical records and communicated to future teams
They are not absolutely binding in emergencies but must be given significant weight
explanation.pearls: The PULSAR study from Melbourne demonstrated that facilitated advance directive completion reduced involuntary treatment episodes by 25% and improved working alliance scores.

subtopic_detail: SSRI use in pregnancy risk counselling
stem: A 30-year-old woman with recurrent major depression stable on sertraline 100 mg daily presents for preconception counselling. She had two previous severe episodes requiring hospitalisation, both occurring after stopping antidepressants. She is planning her first pregnancy. Her current PHQ-9 score is 3 (remission).

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, normal mental state examination

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding sertraline?

options.text: Cease sertraline immediately as all SSRIs are teratogenic in the first trimester
options.rationale: SSRIs are NOT established teratogens. Sertraline has reassuring safety data in pregnancy. Ceasing an effective antidepressant in a patient with severe recurrent depression carries high relapse risk. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Continue sertraline through pregnancy after discussing low absolute neonatal risk
options.rationale: For women with severe recurrent depression who have relapsed upon cessation, continuing sertraline through pregnancy is generally recommended. Sertraline has one of the most reassuring safety profiles among SSRIs. Neonatal adaptation syndrome occurs in approximately 30% but is typically mild. Relapse risk upon cessation is 50-70%. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Switch to paroxetine as it is considered the safest SSRI option in pregnancy
options.rationale: Paroxetine has the MOST concerning pregnancy data among SSRIs with a small increased risk of cardiac malformations. Switching from sertraline to paroxetine is inappropriate. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Taper sertraline over 4 weeks and substitute with St John's wort as a natural alternative
options.rationale: St John's wort is NOT recommended in pregnancy. It has unpredictable pharmacokinetics, significant drug interactions, and minimal safety data. 'Natural' does not equate to safety. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Reduce sertraline to the minimum effective dose and plan to cease by 36 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Ceasing at 36 weeks risks maternal relapse during the highest-risk peripartum period. Neonatal adaptation syndrome is relatively benign and self-limiting. Current guidelines do not recommend routine third-trimester cessation. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

explanation.summary: Antidepressant use in pregnancy requires individualised risk-benefit analysis. For severe recurrent depression, the risks of untreated maternal depression generally outweigh the low absolute risks of SSRI exposure. Sertraline and fluoxetine have the most reassuring data. Neonatal adaptation syndrome is typically mild and self-limiting.
key_points: Sertraline has one of the most reassuring safety profiles among SSRIs in pregnancy
Neonatal adaptation syndrome occurs in approximately 30% but is typically mild
Paroxetine has the most concerning pregnancy data among SSRIs
Cessation of effective antidepressants in recurrent depression carries 50-70% relapse risk
explanation.pearls: The absolute risk increase for persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn with late-pregnancy SSRI exposure is approximately 1-2 per 1000 above the baseline rate of 1-2 per 1000.

sources.label: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

subtopic_detail: Lithium management in pregnancy - peripartum dosing
stem: A 33-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has continued lithium throughout pregnancy after shared decision making. She is now 36 weeks gestation and stable. Her most recent lithium level is 0.7 mmol/L. Her renal and thyroid function are normal. She is planning a vaginal delivery. Her previous manic episodes were severe, requiring ICU admission during one episode.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 82, normal foetal heart rate
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.7 mmol/L, TSH 2.8 mIU/L, creatinine 58 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate lithium management strategy around delivery?

options.text: Continue current lithium dose through delivery and postpartum without changes
options.rationale: Renal lithium clearance increases by 50-100% during pregnancy. At delivery, GFR drops acutely, risking toxicity if the pregnancy dose is maintained. Dose adjustment is essential. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Cease lithium at 36 weeks to avoid neonatal toxicity and restart 48 hours postpartum
options.rationale: Ceasing lithium leaves the mother unprotected during the highest-risk period for bipolar relapse. The early postpartum period carries 25-50% relapse risk in bipolar I. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Reduce lithium to the pre-pregnancy dose at onset of labour and monitor levels closely postpartum
options.rationale: Reduce to the pre-pregnancy dose at onset of labour to account for the abrupt GFR decline at delivery. Monitor levels within 24 hours of delivery and twice weekly for 2 weeks. Adequate hydration during labour is essential. Lithium should be withheld during dehydration. Lithium must not be ceased entirely due to extremely high postpartum relapse risk. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Switch from lithium to sodium valproate at 36 weeks for safer peripartum management
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is absolutely contraindicated in women of reproductive age due to teratogenicity. Switching during pregnancy is inappropriate. Medication switches during the peripartum period also increase relapse risk. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Increase lithium dose by 25% from 36 weeks to ensure therapeutic levels during delivery stress
options.rationale: Increasing dose would exacerbate post-delivery toxicity risk when GFR drops acutely. The opposite - dose reduction - is needed. (Source: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

explanation.summary: Lithium pharmacokinetics change significantly during pregnancy. At delivery, GFR returns to baseline abruptly, requiring dose reduction. The recommended strategy is to reduce to the pre-pregnancy dose at onset of labour, maintain hydration, and monitor levels closely postpartum. Lithium should not be ceased entirely due to extremely high relapse risk.
key_points: Renal lithium clearance increases 50-100% during pregnancy
At delivery, GFR drops acutely - dose must be reduced
Monitor lithium levels within 24 hours of delivery and twice weekly for 2 weeks
Never cease lithium entirely peripartum in severe bipolar I
explanation.pearls: The postpartum relapse risk in bipolar I is approximately 25-50% with treatment, rising to 70% or higher if mood stabilisers are ceased - making the early postpartum period the single highest-risk phase.

sources.label: COPE Guideline: Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

subtopic_detail: Fitness to stand trial assessment
stem: A 35-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been charged with serious assault. During psychiatric assessment, he can name his solicitor and understands the charge. However, he holds a fixed delusional belief that his solicitor is conspiring with the prosecution against him and refuses to communicate with his lawyer. He cannot challenge prosecution witnesses because he believes they are government agents. He is on clozapine 400 mg daily with a level of 0.42 mg/L.

data_table.Examination: Persecutory delusions involving legal representatives, mild thought disorder, suspicious and guarded affect, orientated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate opinion regarding fitness to stand trial?

options.text: Fit to stand trial because he understands the charge and can identify his solicitor
options.rationale: Understanding and identifying are necessary but not sufficient. The legal test requires functional capacity to instruct counsel, follow proceedings, and challenge evidence. His delusions prevent this. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: Unfit on permanent basis with recommendation for indefinite forensic order
options.rationale: Fitness is not permanent. Treatment optimisation may improve the clinical picture. The clozapine level (0.42) has room for dose adjustment. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: Fitness assessment is premature; defer until clozapine level is above 0.60 mg/L
options.rationale: The court has requested the assessment. The assessor should provide their opinion on current fitness while noting treatment optimisation may improve the picture. Deferring is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: Currently unfit due to inability to instruct counsel; recommend treatment and reassessment
options.rationale: The Presser criteria require the accused to understand the charge AND be able to instruct counsel, follow proceedings, and challenge evidence. This patient meets some criteria but fails on instructing counsel due to persecutory delusions. The opinion should be 'currently unfit' with recommendation for treatment optimisation and reassessment. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: Fit to stand trial with a recommendation for a support person during proceedings
options.rationale: A support person cannot overcome active psychotic symptoms preventing the lawyer-client relationship. He is currently unfit due to the nature of his symptoms. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Fitness to stand trial (Presser criteria) requires both understanding AND functional capacity to participate in one's defence. Knowledge alone is insufficient. Current unfitness due to treatable illness should include a recommendation for treatment and reassessment.
key_points: Fitness requires both understanding AND functional capacity to instruct counsel and challenge evidence
Presser criteria include ability to instruct counsel, follow proceedings, and challenge evidence
Current unfitness due to treatable illness warrants treatment and reassessment recommendation
Fitness can change with treatment - it is not permanent
explanation.pearls: In Australian jurisdictions, unfitness does not equate to acquittal - the accused may be placed on a forensic order, and if fitness is not restored within 12-24 months, a special hearing determines whether they committed the act.

subtopic_detail: Structured professional judgement in violence risk assessment
stem: A 40-year-old man with schizophrenia is being assessed by the forensic psychiatry team prior to a tribunal review. He was found not guilty by reason of mental impairment for a serious violent offence 5 years ago. He has been in a forensic facility since. His psychosis is well controlled on long-acting injectable aripiprazole. He has completed a violence prevention program. The tribunal has asked for a violence risk assessment.

data_table.Examination: No active psychotic symptoms, good insight, cooperative, expresses remorse

lead_in: Which risk assessment approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Unstructured clinical judgement based on interview and clinical experience
options.rationale: Unstructured clinical judgement has poor predictive validity (AUC approximately 0.55-0.65). It is vulnerable to cognitive biases and cannot be transparently communicated to tribunals. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: Static-99R actuarial tool to quantify recidivism probability
options.rationale: Static-99R is specifically for sexual offence recidivism, not general violence risk. Using the wrong tool would provide misleading information. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: PCL-R (Psychopathy Checklist - Revised) as the primary assessment framework
options.rationale: The PCL-R assesses psychopathic traits, which are a risk factor but do not constitute a comprehensive violence risk assessment. It does not assess dynamic risk factors or protective factors. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: GAF (Global Assessment of Functioning) scale to quantify current functioning
options.rationale: The GAF is a general functioning scale, not a violence risk assessment tool. It does not assess historical risk factors or risk management needs. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

options.text: HCR-20 structured professional judgement framework with scenario planning
options.rationale: The HCR-20 is the gold-standard SPJ tool for violence risk in forensic psychiatric populations. It combines 10 historical, 5 clinical, and 5 risk management factors to inform a formulated judgement with scenario planning, not a numerical probability. It is recommended by RANZCP and is well-suited to tribunal communication. (Source: RANZCP Forensic Psychiatry Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Violence risk assessment has evolved from unstructured judgement through actuarial approaches to structured professional judgement. The HCR-20 is the most widely used SPJ tool, assessing historical, clinical, and risk management factors to inform a formulated judgement with scenario planning.
key_points: Unstructured clinical judgement has poor predictive validity
The HCR-20 is the gold-standard SPJ tool for forensic psychiatric populations
SPJ produces a formulated risk judgement, not a numerical probability
Dynamic clinical factors are most important for conditional release decisions
explanation.pearls: The HCR-20 V3 dynamic clinical items (insight, violent ideation, symptoms, instability, treatment responsiveness) are the most important for conditional release decisions because they identify modifiable targets.

subtopic_detail: BPSD non-pharmacological management
stem: An 82-year-old man with moderate Alzheimer disease in residential aged care has been increasingly agitated in the late afternoon for 3 weeks, pacing and calling out. There is no acute illness, pain, urinary retention, constipation, or medication changes. A urine dipstick and blood tests are unremarkable.

data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal, CRP 4 mg/L, urinalysis negative, BSL 6.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Implement structured afternoon activities, environmental changes, and carer education
options.rationale: BPSD should be managed with non-pharmacological interventions first-line after excluding physical causes. This presentation is consistent with sundowning. Evidence-based approaches include structured activities, environmental modifications, and carer education. Antipsychotics increase mortality 1.5-1.7 fold in dementia. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines: Dementia)

options.text: Commence risperidone 0.25 mg twice daily for behavioural symptom management
options.rationale: Antipsychotics should not be first-line for BPSD. They carry significant risks including increased mortality (1.5-1.7 fold) and stroke (2-3 fold). They should only be considered after non-pharmacological measures have been trialled. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Use in Dementia)

options.text: Add memantine 5 mg daily titrating to 20 mg for behavioural symptoms
options.rationale: Memantine has modest evidence for agitation in moderate-severe AD but is not first-line for BPSD. Non-pharmacological approaches should be implemented first. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guidelines: Dementia)

options.text: Increase donepezil to 23 mg daily to target behavioural symptoms more effectively
options.rationale: Higher-dose donepezil is not approved in Australia at this dose for BPSD. Non-pharmacological interventions are first-line. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence regular lorazepam 0.5 mg three times daily for anxiety-driven agitation
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are generally avoided in dementia due to increased fall risk, paradoxical agitation, sedation, and worsening cognition. They are associated with increased mortality. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Use in Dementia)

explanation.summary: BPSD affects up to 90% of people with dementia. First-line management is always non-pharmacological after excluding physical causes. Antipsychotics increase mortality 1.5-1.7 fold in dementia.
key_points: Non-pharmacological interventions are ALWAYS first-line for BPSD
Exclude physical causes: pain, infection, constipation, delirium, medications
Antipsychotics increase mortality 1.5-1.7 fold and stroke risk 2-3 fold in dementia
Sundowning responds well to structured activities and environmental modification
explanation.pearls: Risperidone is the only antipsychotic with TGA approval for BPSD in Australia, restricted to a maximum of 12 weeks at 0.25-1 mg daily - any use beyond this is off-label.

subtopic_detail: Pseudodementia vs early Alzheimer disease differentiation
stem: A 72-year-old retired teacher presents with her husband who reports 8 months of progressive memory difficulties. She has stopped reading novels because she cannot follow the plot. She is withdrawn and has lost interest in her garden. She has had two previous depressive episodes (ages 48 and 61), both fully remitting with sertraline. On testing, she scores 22/30 on the MoCA. When she cannot recall a word she becomes frustrated and says 'I don't know - I can't remember anything anymore.' She gives multiple 'don't know' responses. Her verbal fluency for animals is 8 in 60 seconds. Her FDG-PET shows bilateral temporoparietal hypometabolism.

data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.4 mIU/L, B12 380 pmol/L, folate 18 nmol/L, MoCA 22/30, GDS-15 score 11/15
data_table.FDG-PET: Bilateral temporoparietal hypometabolism with relative frontal sparing

lead_in: Which feature most strongly suggests the cognitive impairment is not solely attributable to depression?

options.text: The presence of 'don't know' responses on cognitive testing
options.rationale: 'Don't know' responses are actually MORE characteristic of depressive pseudodementia. Alzheimer patients typically attempt answers, producing near-miss confabulations. This feature favours depression. (Source: Lishman's Organic Psychiatry)

options.text: The bilateral temporoparietal hypometabolism on FDG-PET
options.rationale: Bilateral temporoparietal hypometabolism on FDG-PET is a hallmark biomarker pattern for Alzheimer disease, with approximately 85-90% specificity. Depression-related cognitive impairment typically shows frontal or diffuse hypometabolism that normalises after treatment. This finding strongly suggests underlying neurodegenerative pathology alongside the depressive symptoms. (Source: NIA-AA Research Framework)

options.text: The GDS-15 score of 11/15 confirming significant depression
options.rationale: A high GDS-15 confirms depressive symptoms but depression occurs in 30-40% of AD patients. The GDS does not help differentiate whether cognitive impairment is solely depression-driven. (Source: Lishman's Organic Psychiatry)

options.text: The verbal fluency score of 8 animals in 60 seconds
options.rationale: Reduced verbal fluency occurs in both depression and early dementia. A score of 8 is abnormal but not specific enough to differentiate the two. (Source: Lishman's Organic Psychiatry)

options.text: The history of two previous fully remitting depressive episodes
options.rationale: Previous episodes with recovery could support pseudodementia. But late-life depression is also a risk factor for and early manifestation of AD. Previous episodes alone do not differentiate. (Source: Lishman's Organic Psychiatry)

explanation.summary: Differentiating depressive pseudodementia from early AD is one of the most challenging diagnostic problems in old age psychiatry. FDG-PET temporoparietal hypometabolism is specific to Alzheimer pathology and is not a feature of depression alone. 'Don't know' responses actually favour depression. When biomarkers suggest coexisting pathology, both conditions should be treated.
key_points: FDG-PET temporoparietal hypometabolism is specific to Alzheimer pathology
'Don't know' responses favour depression over dementia
Depression and AD coexist in 30-40% of cases
Late-life depression is both a risk factor for and early manifestation of AD
explanation.pearls: The Depressed ADNI cohort showed that late-life depression with concurrent biomarker-positive Alzheimer pathology has a 5-year conversion rate to dementia of approximately 85%, versus 20% for biomarker-negative depressive pseudodementia.
